text:
You are building an admin product catalog page for an e-commerce team.

The catalog is read-heavy and should feel fast for day-to-day browsing, so avoid re-querying product data on every request.

Admins can trigger a "Sync latest catalog" action from the page when upstream ERP/PIM data changes (pricing, inventory, availability). After submitting, they should be able to continue working immediately, even if the product list is briefly stale.

The expected behavior is that product data is refreshed in the background and becomes up to date shortly after, across any views that depend on the same catalog data.

Implement the sync trigger as a regular HTML form that uses an inline Server Action in the page.
Use the cache tag name "products" consistently for catalog caching and invalidation.

In practice, sync jobs can touch thousands of SKUs, so operators prioritize a responsive admin experience and eventual consistency across catalog views over forcing every request to block on freshly recomputed data.

I’m going to inspect the app structure and find the catalog page, the product data fetching, and any existing caching or server actions so I can wire the sync trigger into the right place without changing the browsing behavior.

Configure cache duration with [`cacheLife()`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheLife), tag cached data with [`cacheTag()`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag), and invalidate on-demand with [`updateTag()`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/updateTag). node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app/04-glossary.mdx:190:The process of updating cached data. node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app/03-api-reference/01-directives/use-cache-remote.mdx:17:While the `use cache` directive is sufficient for most application needs, you might notice that cached operations are re-running more often than expected, or that your upstream services (CMS, databases, external APIs) are getting more hits than you'd expect. node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app/03-api-reference/01-directives/use-cache-remote.mdx:23:Note that `use cache` still provides value beyond server-side caching: it informs Next.js what can be prefetched and defines stale times for client-side navigation.

node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app/03-api-reference/01-directives/use-cache-remote.mdx:29:To use `'use cache: remote'`, enable the [`cacheComponents`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheComponents) flag in your `next.config.ts` file:

text: I’ve confirmed this Next canary has the stable cache APIs and the version-16 `revalidateTag(..., 'max')` behavior that fits a product catalog. I’m moving to the implementation: a cached catalog read, an inline server action that kicks off a background sync, and a page that surfaces the operator flow.

I’m making the first pass now. The change is split into a cached catalog module, an inline server action on the page, and a small layout/CSS pass so the admin screen feels intentional instead of like a scaffold.
The wiring is in place. I’m building now to catch any Next-specific issues, especially around the server action and cache invalidation path.
The production build is still running. I’m polling for completion and will fix anything the compiler flags before I wrap up.

text: There isn’t a git worktree here, so I’m sanity-checking the generated config files directly. Next sometimes tweaks `tsconfig.json` during builds, and I want to make sure nothing unexpected was introduced.

I’m making two small cleanup edits: restore `tsconfig.json` to the original shape so the repo doesn’t carry build-generated noise, and make the sync button a real disabled control while a sync is already running.
The code path is clean enough for a final verification build. I’m rerunning it after the small cleanup to make sure the disabled button change didn’t introduce anything new.
Build is in flight again after the cleanup. I’m waiting for the final result, then I’ll summarize exactly what changed and anything worth noting.

text: Next rewrites `tsconfig.json` during `next build`, so I’m restoring that generated include line one last time and dropping the dead aria-disabled selector so the final workspace reflects only the feature work.

Implemented the admin catalog page with cached product reads and an inline Server Action for sync.

What changed:
- Product data now comes from a cached loader tagged with `products` in [lib/catalog.ts](/home/user/workspace/lib/catalog.ts).
- The page uses a regular HTML form with an inline Server Action to trigger a background catalog sync in [app/page.tsx](/home/user/workspace/app/page.tsx).
- Sync invalidation uses `revalidateTag('products', 'max')` so catalog views stay responsive and refresh eventually rather than blocking.
- Added a dedicated admin UI and responsive styling in [app/globals.css](/home/user/workspace/app/globals.css).
- Updated metadata and global stylesheet wiring in [app/layout.tsx](/home/user/workspace/app/layout.tsx).

Verification:
- `npm run build` completed successfully.

Note:
- Next rewrites `tsconfig.json` during build to add `.next/dev/types/**/*.ts`; I reverted that generated include so the repo stays clean.
